sections.heading: MongoDB Atlas or MongoDB Cloud Manager?
sections.body:
Atlas offers a managed and simplified experience. Atlas users
have access to a curated selection of configuration and infrastructure
options. The available Atlas configuration and infrastructure
options may not provide the flexibility that some users require. For
example, Atlas requires TLS (Transport Layer Security) for cluster connectivity and does not
surface options for disabling TLS (Transport Layer Security). Atlas suits users who want
fewer moving parts to manage, enabling developers and database
administrators to be more productive.

MongoDB Cloud Manager offers more control by exposing more configuration options
on the infrastructure of your choice. Cloud Manager users have access to
advanced operations and a higher level of control, but must manage the
full lifecycle of their infrastructure. Cloud Manager is best suited for users
who require a higher level of control over their MongoDB clusters.

For guidance on which MongoDB service best suits the needs of your
organization,
[contact MongoDB Support](https://www.mongodb.com/contact/atlas).

sections.heading: Can I bring an existing MongoDB deployment into MongoDB Atlas for management?
sections.body:
No. However, you can upload data from existing MongoDB deployment into
MongoDB Atlas.

You can use live migration in the Atlas UI to live migrate data from
a source replica set or sharded cluster to an Atlas cluster.
To choose an import strategy, see [Migrate or Import Data](import-strategies).

You can also write scripts using official MongoDB supported drivers to
upload data.

sections.heading: Can I migrate between regions?
sections.body:
Yes. You can change one or more regions for a given cluster within the
original cloud service provider or across different cloud service providers.
MongoDB Atlas uses a rolling-migration strategy for moving nodes from the
original region to a new region to preserve cluster availability.

> **Important:** 
>
> AWS Only
>
> Amazon Web Services (AWS) Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) peering
> connections are region-specific. Clusters utilizing an existing
> VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) peering connection to an AWS (Amazon Web Services) VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) in a given AWS (Amazon Web Services) region
> lose access to that peering connection if moved to a different AWS (Amazon Web Services)
> region. The moved cluster may use an existing peering connection in
> the new region.
>
> To learn more, see Set Up a Network Peering Connection.

If you need to migrate data between regions on different cloud service
providers, you can:

- [edit the cluster configuration](edit-cluster-atlas) in the Atlas UI by changing the cloud providers and region for each cluster whose data you are migrating. The migration rolls cluster by cluster, starting from the secondary nodes.

- [Configure High Availability and Workload Isolation](create-cluster-multi-region). This method minimizes the risk of issues related to cloud provider capacity, but can result in higher [data transfer costs](data-transfer-costs).

- Seed the destination cluster using mongorestore.

- Restore a backup from the source cluster to the destination cluster.

> **Warning:** 
>
> - When you transition to a new cloud provider, your deployment IP addresses change.
>
> - Data migration interrupts VPC peering and private endpoint operation. You must reconfigure these after completing migration.
>
> - Adding or moving a node to a new region or cloud provider without a primary or secondary node
>   requires each newly migrated replica set member to perform an [initial sync](manual/core/replica-set-sync).
>
> - If you created an Atlas cluster on Google Cloud or Microsoft Azure before November 2, 2020 when Atlas added support for
>   multi-cloud clusters, changing to a different provider changes the connection string to your new cluster. Consider
>   scheduling a time to [update your applications with the new connection string](tenant-to-tenant) to connect to the cluster again.

> **See Also:** [Migrate or Import Data](import-strategies)

sections.heading: Does Atlas support cross-region deployments?
sections.body:
Yes. You can specify additional regions for high availability or local
reads when creating or
scaling a deployment.

Atlas supports cross-cloud service provider deployments. To learn
more, see [highest priority region](deploy-across-multiple-regions).

sections.heading: What AWS regions does Atlas support?
sections.body:
Atlas supports all AWS (Amazon Web Services) regions other than those in China and
[US GovCloud](https://aws.amazon.com/govcloud-us/). To learn more,
see Amazon Web Services (AWS).

sections.heading: Can I pause or stop my Atlas clusters?
sections.body:
You can [pause](pause-cluster) an `M10+` paid cluster for up to
30 days at a time. Atlas automatically resumes the cluster after 30 days.

sections.heading: Can I pre-split chunks in a Atlas sharded cluster?
sections.body:
The [Atlas admin](manual/reference/built-in-roles) database user role has the necessary
privileges to pre-split chunks in an empty sharded collection.

To learn more about sharded cluster chunk creation and management, see
[Create Chunks in a Sharded Cluster](manual/tutorial/create-chunks-in-sharded-cluster).

sections.heading: Can MongoDB Atlas deploy clusters with more than 50 shards?
sections.body:
Yes, MongoDB Atlas allows selection of up to 70 shards. If you're
interested in more than 70 shards, contact MongoDB Support.

sections.heading: How does MongoDB Atlas deliver high availability?
sections.body:
Atlas clusters use replication capability of MongoDB to deliver high
availability. All Atlas clusters are either replica sets or sharded
clusters where each shard is a replica set. To learn more about
MongoDB replica sets and replication, see [Replication](manual/replication).

Atlas uses a rolling update strategy for executing maintenance or
infrastructure operations, such as applying security patches or scaling up
an Atlas cluster. The rolling update strategy ensures that the cluster
can process reads and writes for the majority of the maintenance or
infrastructure operation. During the rolling update procedure:

- Atlas applies the changes to each secondary node in the cluster.

- Atlas directs the primary node to step down to the secondary
  state and trigger an election of a new primary.

- Once the cluster has a new primary, Atlas applies the changes to
  the former primary node.

Applications must hold write operations while the cluster elects a new
primary. The cluster can continue to process
[secondary read operations](manual/core/read-preference) during this
period. Elections on Atlas clusters typically complete within a few
seconds. Factors such as network latency may extend the time required for
replica set elections to complete, which in turn affects the amount of time
your cluster may operate without a primary. These factors are dependent
on your particular cluster architecture.

You can enable retryable writes by adding [retryWrites=true](manual/reference/connection-string) to your Atlas
URI connection string. To learn more, see [Retryable Writes](manual/core/retryable-writes).

For `M10+` clusters, Atlas provides a Test Primary Failover feature that allows
you to check that your applications can detect and react to a replica
set election. By designing applications that can seamlessly handle a
replica set election, you no longer have to worry about the underlying
maintenance occurring on your clusters.

Atlas maintenance operations include OS patches and maintenance patches
for the MongoDB database itself. Infrastructure operations include repair
operations required to replace faulty infrastructure, and scheduled
infrastructure replacements, such as changing the cluster tier.

[Contact MongoDB Support](https://www.mongodb.com/contact/atlas) for help with
architecting your application to use MongoDB Atlas with optimal
availability.

sections.heading: What are Analytics Nodes?
sections.body:
*Available on M10+ clusters.*

Analytics nodes are specialized read-only nodes used to isolate
queries which you do not want to affect your operational workload.
They are useful for handling analytic data such as reporting queries
executed by BI (Business Intelligence) tools.

Analytics nodes and [read-only nodes](deploy-read-only-replicas)
are configured with distinct
[pre-defined replica set tags](replica-set-tags) that allow you to direct
queries to desired node types and regions. For details on the
pre-defined replica set tags implemented by Atlas, see
[pre-defined replica set tags](replica-set-tags).

You can have up to 50 total nodes on a multi-region cluster.
Within that [limit](manual/reference/limits)
there's no maximum number of analytics nodes.

Analytics nodes cannot contribute to a cluster's availability because
they cannot participate in [elections](manual/reference/glossary), or become the
[primary](manual/reference/glossary) for their cluster.

sections.heading: Why can't I submit another disk storage change request?
sections.body:
If you recently submitted a disk storage change request, AWS (Amazon Web Services) requires
you to wait 6 hours and for the first request to complete before
submitting another disk change request.

sections.heading: How can I migrate my Atlas cluster from one cloud provider to another?
sections.body:
If you use public IPs to connect to the Atlas cluster from your
application, you can switch to a different cloud provider most efficiently
by modifying the cluster on the Atlas UI. Provided that your application is
resilient to failovers, this should migrate your cluster successfully.

However, if your application is connecting to the Atlas cluster
through [VPC/VNet peering](vpc-peering) or PrivateLink, the peering or
the PrivateLink connection between your application and the cluster breaks
after the cluster migrates to a different cloud provider. Because of
this, you need to consider how your application connects to your cluster
after it is migrated to the new cloud provider. Please consult your
application's network administrator for additional advice or
[MongoDB Support](request-support).

> **Note:** If your cluster contains [Search Nodes](configure-search-nodes),
> you can only migrate to cloud provider regions that support Search
> Nodes. For a full list of cloud provider regions that don't support
> Search Nodes, see [Cloud Provider Regions](fts-cloud-provider-regions).
